critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with genuine attention to leakage, drift, and operational alignment. The temporal split is correct, the feature engineering is disciplined, and the scope claims are appropriately modest. I will focus on the issues I find genuinely problematic rather than manufacturing concerns.

**Issue 1: The 'incident group or time window' query construction for LambdaMART is underdefined, and a misconfiguration here silently degrades the experiment into something much weaker than claimed.**

LambdaMART and listwise rankers require a query/group structure: the model learns to rank items *within a group*, not globally. The document says 'within incident groups or time windows' but leaves the actual construction vague. This matters enormously.

If groups are defined by time windows (e.g., daily batches), then many windows will contain zero or one malicious alert. Pairwise/listwise objectives are undefined or vacuous for such groups—LightGBM silently drops single-item groups. If most windows have no positive example, the ranker trains on a very small fraction of the data and the gradient signal mostly comes from windows with confirmed incidents. The model may then learn to recognize 'incident context' features (e.g., time-of-day patterns correlated with attacker campaigns) rather than learning to rank alert quality. On easy days where an incident occurs among 200 benign alerts, NDCG@10 looks great. On hard days with no confirmed incident, the evaluation is undefined and excluded—making the test set implicitly biased toward incident-rich windows.

If groups are incident-linked (all alerts associated with an incident form one group), the positive rate per group is artificially inflated, the ranker learns a trivially different distribution than production, and the logistic regression baseline is never actually solving the same problem—it sees globally imbalanced data while the ranker sees locally balanced groups.

Evidence for/against: Report the distribution of group sizes and positive rates per group. If >30% of groups have a single item or zero positives, the ranking objective is not doing what's claimed. Compare global NDCG (treating the entire test window as one ranked list) against within-group NDCG—a large discrepancy would reveal that within-group structure is doing most of the work.

**Issue 2: Unlabeled test alerts included with implicit negative treatment create a biased NDCG estimate.**

The design correctly retains unlabeled alerts in the test set to reflect operational reality. However, NDCG computation requires relevance scores for all items in the ranked list. The document does not specify how unlabeled alerts are treated in the metric computation.

If unlabeled alerts are assigned relevance 0 (benign), then confirmed malicious alerts that rank below many unlabeled alerts will be penalized, correctly. But if a true malicious alert was never investigated and therefore unlabeled, it acts as a false negative in the metric: the ranker may correctly surface it, receive no credit, and even be penalized for ranking it above a 'confirmed benign' alert. With 18 months of data from a single SOC, a non-trivial fraction of unlabeled alerts are likely true positives—SOC coverage rates in practice are well below 100%. The NDCG estimate is then a pessimistic lower bound for the ranker and systematically biased against surfacing novel or low-volume attack patterns that were never followed up.

This is not merely a conservatism concern—it creates a differential bias between the ranker and the classifier. A ranker that learns to surface novel patterns (the stated goal) will be penalized more than a classifier that memorizes common rule signatures, because the novel true positives are disproportionately in the unlabeled set.

Evidence for/against: Estimate the unlabeled-alert true-positive rate using a held-out audit: have an analyst manually disposition a random sample of 100–200 unlabeled test alerts. If >5% are true positives, the metric is materially biased. Alternatively, report NDCG computed only on alerts with confirmed dispositions and compare to the full-test NDCG to measure the magnitude of the discrepancy.

**Issue 3: The 'historical disposition rate' feature is a target-encoded label statistic and requires extremely careful scope to avoid lookahead in a non-stationary environment.**

The document says historical disposition rates are 'computed on training data only.' In a temporally split experiment this is necessary but not sufficient. The disposition rate for a given rule ID is computed from months 1–9. If a rule was introduced in month 6, its disposition rate is computed from only 3 months of data and has high variance—a single incident inflates the rate dramatically. When this feature is used on validation (months 10–12) or test (months 13–18) data, the rate reflects early-deployment behavior that may not generalize as the rule matures or as attackers adapt.

More critically: if a rule was retired or modified during the training period, the disposition rate aggregates behavior across different rule versions, injecting noise. For new rules first appearing in validation or test, this feature has no training-set entry and falls through to imputation (mean/mode of training disposition rates), which is a coarse fallback that may mask the model's inability to handle novel rules—exactly the hardest and most important case for SOC triage.

This is a precondition failure: the ranking model's supposed advantage over the classifier should come from learning to generalize across alert types. If novel rules fall back to a mean imputation for the disposition-rate feature, both models are effectively running blind on new rules, and the comparison does not test the core hypothesis.

Evidence for/against: Stratify test performance by rule age (rules seen in training vs. rules first appearing in test). If performance on novel rules is near-random or indistinguishable between ranker and classifier, the experiment does not establish the claimed generalization advantage. Report the fraction of test alerts belonging to rules not present in training—if this exceeds 20%, the novel-rule behavior dominates the comparison.

**Summary**

The three issues share a common root: the evaluation assumes that the ranking problem as formulated (group structure, label completeness, feature coverage) matches the operational deployment scenario being claimed. Issue 1 is the most fundamental—if the query group construction is misconfigured, the ranker is not solving the stated problem. Issue 2 creates systematic metric bias against the model's intended behavior. Issue 3 is a precondition failure for the core generalization claim. The design is otherwise disciplined, and the ablation controls are thoughtful, but these three issues require explicit resolution before the comparison is valid.
all_issues_raised: The LambdaMART query/group construction is underdefined: time-window groups with zero or one positive silently make the ranking objective vacuous for most of the data, and incident-linked groups inflate per-group positive rates in ways that make the ranker and classifier incomparable.

Unlabeled test alerts assigned implicit relevance 0 create systematic metric bias against a ranker that surfaces uninvestigated true positives, which is disproportionately the novel-pattern case the experiment is designed to test.

The historical disposition rate feature falls back to mean imputation for rules not seen in training, making both models effectively blind on novel rules and preventing the experiment from testing the core generalization claim where it matters most.
all_issues_adjudicated: The LambdaMART query/group construction is underdefined: time-window groups with zero or one positive silently make the ranking objective vacuous for most of the data, and incident-linked groups inflate per-group positive rates in ways that make the ranker and classifier incomparable.

Unlabeled test alerts assigned implicit relevance 0 create systematic metric bias against a ranker that surfaces uninvestigated true positives, which is disproportionately the novel-pattern case the experiment is designed to test.

The historical disposition rate feature falls back to mean imputation for rules not seen in training, making both models effectively blind on novel rules and preventing the experiment from testing the core generalization claim where it matters most.
